event_name: UFC Fight Night: Kape vs. Horiguchi

fights.fighter_a_bio: Kape hunts with lightning-fast combos and relies on his hand speed to overwhelm opponents early. Problem: he gasses if he can't finish quick and gets picked apart by patient wrestlers who smother his offense.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Horiguchi mixes slick footwork with sudden takedowns—he'll strike you, then drag you down when you bite on fakes. He's the clear favorite here (83.4% by our model) because he controls pace and has answers for Kape's speed bursts.
fights.watch_for: Can Kape land clean and back Horiguchi up in round one, or does Horiguchi lock down the clinch early and establish wrestling control?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Nascimento is a heavy pressure fighter who mixes striking combinations with opportunistic takedowns—he doesn't commit to one game plan, which keeps opponents guessing. Our model has him as a 90% favorite, and his 1534 Elo rating reflects consistent execution against varied opponents.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Raposo is a grind-it-out wrestler who hunts leg kicks and uses clinch work to set up takedowns from range. He's the clear underdog here (10% per our model) with a 1468 Elo, suggesting he struggles against strikers who keep distance.
fights.watch_for: Can Raposo land early leg kicks and clinch control to slow Nascimento's pace, or will Nascimento's striking volume keep him out of grab range long enough to coast to a decision?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Cutelaba hunts takedowns early and often, using his wrestling to control pace and drain energy from opponents on the mat. He's prone to eating strikes on the way in and gets caught in scrambles—his chain wrestling doesn't always connect cleanly against slick opponents.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Stirling mixes striking combinations with timing takedowns, using footwork to stay outside before closing distance on his terms. He's the model's heavy pick (70%) because he controls distance better than Cutelaba and doesn't get desperate with his shot selection.
fights.watch_for: Whether Cutelaba can land his first takedown before Stirling settles into his striking range—if Stirling plants his feet and starts throwing, Cutelaba gets picked apart.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Andre Lima pressures with heavy hands and uses footwork to cut off the cage—he's a proven finisher who accumulates damage round after round. His Elo rating (1559.8) reflects consistent wins against legitimate competition, and our model gives him a dominant 98.4% edge here.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Kevin Borjas mixes strikes and takedown attempts but hasn't shown the finishing power or submission rate needed to trouble Lima's defense. His lower Elo (1471.9) signals he's facing a significant step up in competition.
fights.watch_for: If Borjas lands an early takedown and keeps Lima on his back for 5+ minutes, he buys time to avoid the stand-up war—but Lima's cardio and scrambling ability make this a long shot.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Hyder Amil lands hard but narrow—he's a pure striker who lives and dies by his hands and footwork. He'll struggle if Rodriguez closes the distance and grinds him on the cage or takes him down.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Christian Rodriguez is the heavy favorite here (82.7%) because he can dictate where the fight happens—he'll pressure Amil, mix in takedowns, and smother his striking. His Elo edge and fight IQ give him control.
fights.watch_for: If Amil catches Rodriguez coming in with a clean combo in the first round, he can steal momentum. Otherwise, Rodriguez's ability to close the gap and work inside will steadily break down Amil's striking game.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bia Mesquita is operating at the same skill level as her opponent according to our model, with an Elo rating essentially tied at 1500.0. Limited fight history makes her a true wildcard—expect this to come down to execution on the night.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Melissa Mullins sits at a marginally higher Elo (1500.1) and edges our model's prediction at 50%, suggesting slightly more proven results in her record. She and Mesquita are nearly identical in terms of predictability, making this a genuine toss-up.
fights.watch_for: Whether either fighter can establish their primary offense early—the fighter who lands their best weapon first (striking volume, clinch control, or takedown timing) will likely dictate pace and pull ahead on the scorecards.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Jhonata Diniz is a slight favorite here with a 54.7% edge according to our model, backed by a higher Elo rating (1532.7) that suggests more proven fight results. Our data leans toward him, but this matchup has moderate unpredictability (0.5 chaos index), so expect some surprises.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jose Montanha sits at 1500.0 Elo with a 45.3% win probability in our model's eyes—a statistical underdog but close enough that the right gameplan could flip this. Like Diniz, he brings mid-range unpredictability to this bout.
fights.watch_for: Early clinch control and who lands first with volume—both fighters have similar baseline records, so whoever dictates the pace in rounds 1-2 likely takes the decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Rosa pressures forward with heavy hands and looks to crack opponents on the way in, but her wrestling defense can break down against sustained takedown attempts. She's most dangerous in the first two rounds before cardio becomes a factor.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Santos uses footwork to stay just outside Rosa's power range, then counters with precise striking or shoots for takedowns when Rosa lunges. Our model gives her a 59.5% edge—she's the slightly more proven fighter and controls the pace better.
fights.watch_for: Whether Rosa can close distance and land clean before Santos establishes her range and starts picking her apart with counters. If Santos gets Rosa to the mat in round 2, she likely coasts to a decision.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Bolanos mixes his striking with takedown attempts, but he's not overly proven at this level—his Elo rating sits below 1500. He'll need to land early and control the pace, otherwise he could get picked apart on the feet.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Aswell is a pure striker who lives at distance, using footwork and timing to land clean shots without trading. Our model gives him a 2-to-1 edge, and his higher Elo suggests he's fought better competition and done it well.
fights.watch_for: Whether Bolanos can close the distance and drag Aswell into the clinch early—if Aswell keeps the fight at range past round 2, Bolanos' cardio and wrestling become less relevant.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Baghdasaryan operates with a slight edge in proven track record (Elo: 1514.7), giving him the model's slight nod at 52.1%. He'll need to dictate pace and positioning to convert that advantage into a decision.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Magomedov sits just behind on paper (Elo: 1500.0), and this matchup has roughly even chaos—meaning either fighter can break the script. He thrives when fights get messy and unpredictable.
fights.watch_for: Whether Baghdasaryan can control the clinch and cage work early. If Magomedov survives the first two rounds and drags this into deep water, the unpredictability (0.5 chaos on both sides) favors the underdog.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Basharat is the model's pick at 59%, riding a higher Elo rating (1563) that suggests he's proven himself against stiffer competition. He'll likely control this fight through pressure and positioning, but the chaos index of 0.5 means Ewing has room to land something unexpected.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ewing comes in as the underdog at 41% win probability with a lower Elo (1500), indicating less consistent results against quality opponents. He'll need to create angles and capitalize on mistakes rather than trade in the middle—that's his path to an upset.
fights.watch_for: Whether Basharat can maintain forward pressure and dictate range, or if Ewing stays loose enough to slip shots and counter. If this turns into a scramble, the fight gets volatile—exactly where Ewing's chances improve.
